column32: Any parameter derived from the Patlak graphical analysis, which models systems with irreversible trapping of the tracer.
The slope of the linear phase of the Patlak plot, representing the macroscopic net irreversible uptake rate of the tracer from plasma into the tissue.
The y-intercept of the Patlak plot's linear phase, representing the distribution volume of the tracer in the rapidly equilibrating compartment (plasma and reversible tissue).
Any value describing the rate constant of movement between specific, defined physiological compartments (e.g., blood, reversible tissue, trapped tissue).
The rate constant describing the transport of unbound tracer from the plasma/vascular compartment into the reversible tissue compartment.
The rate constant describing the reverse transport (washout) of unbound tracer from the reversible tissue compartment back into the plasma compartment.
The rate constant describing the binding or irreversible trapping of tracer within the tissue compartment (e.g., phosphorylation or receptor binding).
The rate constant describing the release of trapped or metabolized tracer from the non-reversible tissue pool back into the reversible tissue pool.
The macroscopic rate constant describing the transport or flow of tracer from plasma to tissue, often interpreted as blood flow when the extraction is high.
The total space occupied by the tracer at equilibrium, defined as the ratio of total tracer concentration in tissue to the free concentration in plasma.
A measure of receptor availability, often defined as the ratio of specifically bound radioligand to the free radioligand concentration in the tissue.
The ratio of the total tracer concentration in a specific binding region to that in a non-binding reference region at equilibrium.
The fraction of tracer delivered to an organ via arterial blood that is subsequently taken up by the organ in a single pass.
The net influx rate, representing the overall irreversible uptake of the tracer.
The volume flow rate of blood through the vasculature of a tissue or organ per unit mass of the tissue.
The volume of blood supplied to a mass of tissue per unit time (typically ml/min/g).
The flow rate of blood through the renal arteries and vessels.
The flow rate of blood through the coronary vasculature per unit mass of myocardial tissue.
